payload.answer: Store the 500-row CSV as a bulk/external artifact and add only minimal metadata/indexes in durable memory; avoid creating individual memory cards for each row unless specific rows or semantic facts are explicitly requested.
payload.decision: Store the CSV as an external/bulk data artifact (attachment/blob) with minimal extracted indexes/metadata in memory; do not create 500 durable per-row memory cards unless the user explicitly requests specific rows or semantic facts to be remembered.

entity_resolution.reason: The fixture describes a generic large CSV table without unique, stable entity identifiers beyond a repeated id/name/value pattern; no specific row facts were provided to resolve into durable entities.

payload.intent: Decide how to store a large CSV table (500 rows) in a personal memory system to support efficient retrieval while avoiding excessive per-row durable memory.

memory_cards.statement: For large tabular inputs (e.g., a CSV with ~500 rows), store the full data as an external/bulk artifact and keep only lightweight derived metadata/indexes in memory rather than creating one durable memory card per row.

Create targeted memory entries only for row-level details that are explicitly requested to be remembered (e.g., specific ids/rows or semantic summaries), and otherwise rely on the artifact plus indexes for lookup.

details: Applied a bulk-data storage policy suitable for large tabular inputs.
Avoided committing ambiguous per-row facts as durable memory.

repair_options: If the user requests specific rows, create memory cards only for those selected rows and link them to the artifact id.
If queries will frequently filter by id/name/value, store summary indexes (e.g., sorted/hashed mappings) in the artifact metadata and keep memory entries limited to index descriptions.
If semantic meaning is needed, store a short generated summary of the dataset plus a provenance link to the artifact; keep raw rows external.
